elements.sent-2411-001: The S-measure construction from nonstandard analysis is used to prove an extension of a result on the intersection of sets in a finitely-additive measure space.
elements.sent-2411-002: This is then used to give a density-limit version of a representation theorem of Banach.
elements.sent-2411-003: The starting point for this note is the following result of Banach (described in \cite{diestelswart as ``marvelous"): \begin{Thm Let $X$ be a set, $B(X)$ be all bounded real functions on $X$, and $\{f_n : n\in\mathbb{N}\}$ be a uniformly bounded sequence.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: The following are equivalent: (i)~$\{f_n\}_n$ converges weakly to $0$; (ii)~for any sequence $\{x_k : k\in\mathbb{N}\}$ in $X$, $\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}\liminf\limits_{k\to\infty f_n(x_k)=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-005: The contrapositive is interesting: \begin{Cor Let $X$ be a set, $B(X)$ be all bounded real functions on $X$, and $\{f_n : n\in\mathbb{N}\}$ be a uniformly bounded sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-006: The following are equivalent: (i)~there is a positive linear functional $T$ on $B(X)$, an infinite set $I\subseteq\N$, and an $r>0$ such that $|T(f_n)|>r$ for every $n\in I$; (ii)~there exists a sequence $\{x_k : k\in\mathbb{N}\}$ in $X$, an infinite set $I\subseteq\N$, and an $r>0$ such that $\liminf\limits_{k\to\infty |f_n(x_k)|>r$ for every $n\in I$.}
elements.sent-2411-007: In this paper we adapt the proof from Ross~\cite{rossbanach to prove a version of the Banach theorem in which $I$ is required to have positive upper density.}
elements.sent-2411-008: For $I\subseteq\N$ let $\bar{d (I)=\limsup_n\|I\cap\{1,2,\dots,n\}\|/n$ (the upper asymptotic density of $I$).}
elements.sent-2411-009: The main result of this paper is the following.
elements.sent-2411-010: The following are equivalent: (i)~there is a positive linear functional $T$ on $B(X)$, an infinite set $I\subseteq\N$ with $\bar{d (I)>0$, and an $r>0$ such that $|T(f_n)|>r$ for every $n\in I$; (ii)~there exists a sequence $\{x_k : k\in\mathbb{N}\}$ in $X$, a set $I\subseteq\N$ with $\bar{d (I)>0$, and an $r>0$ such that $\liminf\limits_{k\to\infty |f_n(x_k)|>r$ for every $n\in I$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Write $d\lim_na_n=L$ provided it is not the case that there is an $r>0$ and set $I\subseteq\N$ with $\bar{d (I)>0$ such that $|f(n)-L|>r$ for all $n\in I$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Then result Theorem~\ref{thm4 becomes: \begin{Cor Let $X$ be a set, $B(X)$ be all bounded real functions on $X$, and $\{f_n : n\in\mathbb{N}\}$ be a uniformly bounded sequence.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The following are equivalent: (i)~$\{f_n\}_n$ weakly d-converges to $0$; (ii)~for any sequence $\{x_k : k\in\mathbb{N}\}$ in $X$, $d\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}\liminf\limits_{k\to\infty f_n(x_k)=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Our proof uses nonstandard analysis, notably Abraham Robinson's notion of S-measurability, and relies on a generalization (Corollary~\ref{bergelson ) to finitely-additive measures of a lemma from Bergelson \cite{bergelson .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The main idea is to substitute Corollary~\ref{bergelson for the weaker \cite[Theorem 1]{rossbanach in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm1 in Ross \cite{rossbanach .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Nonstandard analysis has proved itself increasingly useful for the study of densities; see, for example, Jin \cite{Jin .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The reader is assumed to be familiar with nonstandard analysis in general, and the Loeb measure construction in particular, for example as in Ross \cite{Ross96 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Assume that we work in a nonstandard model in the sense of Robinson, and that this model is as saturated as it needs to be to carry out all constructions; in particular, it is an enlargement.
elements.sent-2411-019: If $(X, \SA, \mu)$ is a finite measure space then both ${^*\SA}$ and $\SA_0=\{{^*A :A\in\SA\}$ are algebras on $^*X$.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Let $\SAS$ be the smallest $\sigma$-algebra\ containing ${\SA_0}$ and $\SAL$ be the smallest $\sigma$-algebra\ containing ${^*\SA}$.
elements.sent-2411-021: By either an appeal to the Carath\'eodory Extension Theorem or an elementary direct construction, $^{\circ* {\mu}$ can be extended to a countably-additive measure (the Loeb measure) $\mu_L$ on $(^*X, \SAL)$, and by restriction on $(^*X, \SAS)$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The algebra $\SAS$ of S-measurable sets was introduced by Robinson \cite{Robinson , then studied later by Henson and Wattenburg \cite{HW (who used S-measurability to understand Egoroff's Theorem), and more recently by the author \cite{Ross96,R1,R2,Ross16 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The main result we need is the following: \begin{Lemma (Henson and Wattenburg, 1981) $\forall A\in\SA_S,\ $ \begin{equation}\mu_L(A) =\inf\{\mu(B)\ :\ A\subseteq{^*B}, B\in\SA\} =\sup\{\mu(B)\ :\ {^*B}\subseteq A,\ B\in{\SA}\} =\mu(A\cap X).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-024: This section gives a new proof of a modest generalization (Corollary~\ref{bergelson ) of a lemma of Bergelson~\cite{bergelson .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Bergelson's result is usually proved using Fatou's Lemma or the Lebesgue Dominated Convergence Theorem; the proof here replaces these with an appeal to Lemma~\ref{smeas .}
elements.sent-2411-026: While this proof is not shorter than the standard ones, it is more explicit, which could prove useful in extending results which use it, such as Furstenburg's Multiple Ergodic Theorem \cite{Furstenberg .}
elements.sent-2411-027: We begin with a weak form of the lemma.
elements.sent-2411-028: For some $I\subseteq\N$ with $\bar{d (I)\ge a, \{A_n\}_{n\in I}$ has the finite intersection property.\end{Lemma Before proceeding with the proof, we note two immediate corollaries.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The first is Bergelson's original result, the second is the extension we need.
elements.sent-2411-030: For some $I\subseteq\N$ with $\bar{d (I)\ge a$ and every finite $J\subseteq I$, $\mu(\bigcap_{n\in J A_n)>0$.\end{Cor.}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Let $A_n'=A_n\setminus B$, where \begin{equation}B=\bigcup\{\bigcap_{i\in J}A_i : J\subseteqN, J finite, \mu(\bigcap_{i\in J}A_i)=0 \}.\end{equation}.
